articles.title: WHAT HAPPENED TO MY TRAFFIC?
articles.summary: Chegg sued Google after losing millions of visitors to the new AI Overviews feature, which seems to deliver Chegg-like homework answers right on Google. Traffic dropped 34%, sparking the first lawsuit of its kind. Others are feeling the impact, too—Stack Overflow and HubSpot also saw steep dips, while The New York Times thrived, possibly thanks to a lucrative licensing deal. It's a sign that AI-driven search may transform how users discover content, and companies without special partnerships could watch their traffic fade.

articles.title: VENTURE PSYCHOLOGY
articles.summary: Early-stage investing is more about the founder's grit than fancy projections. Investors look for relentless, problem-obsessed individuals who will pivot, iterate, and outwork everyone else. History shows that markets shift, products evolve, and competition pops up—what truly matters is whether the founder will quit or not. That unstoppable mindset, plus a willingness to adapt, often outweighs even the best-drafted business plan or pitch deck.

articles.title: EARLY STAGE BRAND DESIGN
articles.summary: Don't overinvest in visual rebranding - focus on product and market fit instead. Early-stage startups should aim for a "good enough" brand design and avoid expensive and lengthy design projects. The key is building something that solves a problem and that people want.

articles.title: MISTAKES IN DUE DILIGENCE THAT KILL DEALS
articles.summary: Loads of founders think a term sheet is the hard part—until investors start combing through the numbers. Sloppy financials, mismatched growth metrics, or a chaotic cap table can scare off potential backers. Failure to address legal snags, compliance issues, or fuzzy IP rights also sinks deals fast. Getting everything buttoned-up and transparent is often the difference between a successful close and a last-minute collapse.

articles.title: AN OPERATOR'S GUIDE TO PRODUCT STRATEGY
articles.summary: Two complementary layers shape a great product roadmap: a near-term “small S” strategy to fix current issues, and a longer-term “big S” vision that imagines the product's future three to ten years out. Collecting user data, brainstorming, and building quick prototypes helps teams converge on realistic plans for now and aspirational plans for later. The key is to force disciplined choices about where you'll focus finite resources—and designing with both immediate and eventual goals in mind.

articles.title: "STEALTH MODE" AND OTHER F'ING BRILLIANT STRATEGIES
articles.summary: Keep your startup in stealth mode to avoid early competition and distractions. Focus on perfecting your product before launch and don't worry about customer validation. Concentrate on coding and raising funds early while benchmarking against competitors without fearing differentiation.

articles.title: COHERENCE
articles.summary: Synthetic ground truth data for AI applications.

articles.title: PEPPR
articles.summary: Convert scattered knowledge from all your existing tools into an AI-powered, self-improving knowledge hub.

articles.title: AGORA API (PRODUCT)
articles.summary: Agora API enables developers to search, purchase, and track orders for 10 million products sold on 25,000 Shopify and WooCommerce stores.

articles.title: FIVE EMERGING VC SWIMLANES
articles.summary: The venture market is fracturing into distinct tiers: tiny pre-seed funds, inception-stage firms, new Series A specialists, mid-sized “too big but not big enough” players, and supersized billion-dollar platforms. Each swimlane has different fund sizes, risk appetites, and target exit multiples. Founders need to know which lane their potential investors occupy so they can raise capital from partners whose portfolio strategy—and check size—truly aligns with their startup's stage and ambitions.

articles.title: THE IMPACT OF FOUNDER PERSONALITIES ON STARTUP SUCCESS
articles.summary: Research shows that startup founders' personality traits significantly impact their ventures' success. Specific Big Five traits like adventurousness and openness are more common in successful founders. Startups with diverse personality types among their founding teams have higher chances of success.

articles.title: IS GROWTH STILL GOOD?
articles.summary: AI companies are reaching $40-80M in revenue faster than ever, with startups like Harvey hitting $50M ARR in just three years. However, rapid growth may actually signal vulnerability - without network effects or unique tech advantages, these companies are often finding "shallow gold" that competitors can easily replicate. Metrics that used to prove success now raise questions about defensibility in a market flooded with corporate innovation budgets.

articles.title: JOE LONSDALE EXPLAINS THE “ONE THING” RULE HE LEARNED FROM PETER THIEL
articles.summary: Founders who chase too many revenue streams usually end up chasing their tails.

articles.title: LONG-TERM SELFISH (AND THE CIRCLES OF US AND NOW)
articles.summary: Selfishness can be short-term—like a toddler scribbling on the couch—or it can be mature and long-term, taking into account the future and a wider circle of people.

articles.title: HERE ARE THE TYPES OF AI COMPANIES ENTERPRISE VCS WANT TO BACK IN 2025
articles.summary: VCs are focusing on AI solutions for specific enterprise tasks, emphasizing application-focused companies that solve unique challenges.
